title: U146: Bennett integration test for stated vision absent (controlled ∘ reversible_compile)
description:
Unified catalogue: U146 — reviews/2026-04-21/UNIFIED_CATALOGUE.md (MEDIUM section)

Source reports: #02 F15
Files: reviews/2026-04-21/02_vision_scope.md

Sites: project-wide (no integration test for stated vision)

Claim: No integration test exercises the stated long-term vision: `controlled ∘ reversible_compile` end-to-end through a small statevector simulator (the Sturm.jl `when(qubit) do f(x) end` use case).

Fix: Add a small integration test that compiles a plain Julia function, controls it, and runs it through a small-N statevector simulator; assert unitarity / correctness on known amplitudes.
close_reason: New test_6u9q_quantum_vision_integration.jl: 21 asserts exercising the Sturm.jl vision end-to-end. Compiles a tiny Bool-to-Bool function (x -> !x with bit_width=1 → 8-wire circuit), wraps via controlled(), and treats the result as a 2^N=256 unitary on a complex statevector. Three coverage axes: (1) for every (ctrl, x) basis-state pair, applies the gates as permutation matrices to a basis-state ket and verifies the unique nonzero output amplitude == 1.0 + 0im AND that the read-out output bit equals classical simulate(cc, ctrl, x); (2) random complex superposition is norm-preserved through the full circuit (proves unitarity within atol 1e-10); (3) the canonical |0,x=0,0> + |1,x=0,0> superposition produces the entangled |0,0,0> + |1,0,1> exactly — the Sturm 'when(qubit) do f(x) end' semantic. Avoided LinearAlgebra stdlib (not in test extras) by inlining a 5-line _norm2 helper; uses Test+Bennett only. _apply_gate_to_statevector! permutes basis-state indices (NOT/CNOT/Toffoli are each permutation matrices) so no 256x256 dense matrix materialised. Pkg.test green: 72928/72931 (3 broken intentionally). Cumulative test count >70k post-grind.

title: U98: controlled.jl assumes contiguous wire allocation (no invariant assert)

close_reason: Added explicit contiguous-wire-allocation invariant assert in src/controlled.jl. The ReversibleCircuit constructor partitions input/output/ancilla over 1:n_wires but does NOT cross-check that the gate stream stays within that range — a malformed circuit with a NOTGate(n_wires+1) would silently collide with the ctrl_wire we choose at n_wires+1. New test_pksz_controlled_contiguous_wires.jl: 13 asserts covering happy path on the canonical i8 x+1 baseline (ctrl_wire == n_wires+1, ctrl=true→f(x), ctrl=false→0), rejection path (NOTGate(99) with n_wires=3 errors with the bead reference in the message), and the empty-gates edge case. close_reason: Replaced bare typemax(Int)÷2 / typemax(Int)÷4 magic in src/pebbling.jl with two named module-scoped consts: _PEBBLE_INF (init sentinel for 'no finite cost yet') and _PEBBLE_FINITE_BOUND (gate threshold below which a cell holds a real cost). 6 sites cleaned across knill_pebble_cost (2 sites) and knill_split_point (4 sites) — all four for-loops now use the names directly. New test_069e_pebble_sentinels.jl: 19 asserts pinning sentinel arithmetic, Knill 1995 base cases (F(1,s)=1, F(n,1)=∞, F(2,2)=3), and min_pebbles/knill_split_point sanity. Pkg.test green.

description:

Claim: Load-bearing invariants lack WHY comments: QCLA depth formula is unasserted; HAMT `is_occupied + is_new == 1` disjoint-one-hot is undocumented; Okasaki RB post-insert invariant unstated; parallel-adder A3 reverse-uncompute proof missing; popcount cost unexplained.

Fix: Add WHY comments (and, where possible, `@assert` checks) at each site, citing the paper/lemma that establishes the invariant.
close_reason: Added WHY comments + paper citations at the 5 load-bearing invariants the bead identified. (1) src/qcla.jl: docstring now cites Draper et al. 2004 §4.1 Theorem 1 + cross-references the test/test_qcla.jl:163-180 regression baseline that pins the 5 cost formulas at canonical W ∈ {4,8,16,32,64}. (2) src/persistent/research/hamt.jl line 134-135: added one-hot-disjoint comment + @assert is_occupied + is_new == 1 explaining why the branchless mux pattern depends on the invariant. (3) src/persistent/research/okasaki_rbt.jl line 274: added Okasaki 1999 (JFP 9(4)) citation + the two RB invariants the post-insert balance restores. (4) src/parallel_adder_tree.jl line 39-58 docstring: added a full proof sketch for why the reverse-level uncompute schedule is correct (each adder's inputs at level d are still live when its replay starts because level d-1's replay happens later; the paper's level-d-2 schedule is unsafe as documented in WORKLOG 2026-04-14). (5) src/persistent/research/popcount.jl: added gate-cost annotation citing the four 32-bit adds and ~248 Toffoli baseline post-U27. All five sites cite the bead reference (Bennett-d1ee / U141) so future grep finds them. Pkg.test green.

New test_uinn_catch_narrowing.jl: static-inspects the source file; every 'catch' line in ir_extract.jl is followed within ≤3 lines by an 'InterruptException' guard. Pkg.test green; no semantic change to LLVM-introspection failure handling.

description:

Fix: Correct the docstring to match the implementation (or fix the implementation if the docstring is the intended spec). Anchor the gate count with a regression test.
close_reason: Corrected lower_add_cuccaro! docstring in src/adder.jl. Old text advertised 2n Toffoli / 5n CNOT / 2n NOT (the original Cuccaro 2004 paper's carry-out variant); actual implementation is the mod-2^W carry-suppressed variant emitting 2W-2 Toffoli, 4W-2 CNOT, 0 NOT, total 6W-4 gates per W. Measurements verified across W in {2,3,4,8,16,32,64}: every formula holds exactly. Docstring now states the corrected formulas with attribution to Bennett-op6a/U140 + 2026-04-26 measurement date + a one-line note explaining the divergence from the paper. New test_op6a_cuccaro_gate_count.jl: 30 asserts pin Toffoli=2W-2, CNOT=4W-2, NOT=0, total=6W-4 across the seven canonical widths plus a W=1 fallback-to-lower_add! sanity check. Pkg.test green standalone.

title: U136: soft_round (roundToIntegralTiesToEven) not implemented
description:
Unified catalogue: U136 — reviews/2026-04-21/UNIFIED_CATALOGUE.md (MEDIUM section)

close_reason: Implemented soft_round (IEEE 754 roundToIntegralTiesToEven) in src/softfloat/fround.jl. Branchless bit-twiddle algorithm: special-cases NaN/Inf/subnormal/|x|<0.5/|x|=0.5/|x|in(0.5,1.0)/|x|>=2^52, then for |x| in [1.0, 2^52) computes round-bit + sticky + ties-to-even via mantissa LSB-after-truncation. Registered in _CALLEES_FP_ROUND so reversible_compile can lower Base.round(::SoftFloat). Added Base.round(::SoftFloat) dispatch. 5,091 new asserts in test_2hhx_soft_round.jl: ties (positive/negative/large), non-tie cases, subnormals→±0, ±Inf passthrough, NaN-with-payload quietened, sNaN force-quieted, carry-into-exp (round 1.999...→2.0), boundary at 2^52, plus 5,000 random raw-bits sweep — bit-exact vs Base.round(::Float64). All 90,041 tests pass.

description:

close_reason: Investigated, doc-only close — bead's claims are largely stale. (1) IRCall arg_widths invariant: constructor at src/ir_types.jl:188 already validated length match, ret_width >= 1, and arg_widths[i] >= 1 prior to this bead. (2) lower_divrem! bypasses _assert_arg_widths_match: STALE — lower_divrem! at src/lower.jl:1825 constructs IRCall with arg_widths=[64,64] and dispatches via lower_call! (line 1872), which unconditionally calls _assert_arg_widths_match (line 2355). A tighter upper bound (<=64 per Bennett-zmw3) was prototyped and reverted because NTuple aggregate returns (e.g. NTuple{9,UInt64} = 576-bit ret_width) are legitimately wider than 64. Decision recorded in src/ir_types.jl as a comment block. Pkg.test() byte-identical: 81252 / 2 broken / 0 fail / 3m52s. Pattern: investigation-only, similar disposition to Bennett-3of2.

title: Rust cross-context LLVM parser for .ll ingest
description:
## Context
T5-P5a/P5b (closed 2026-04-21) shipped `extract_parsed_ir_from_ll` / `extract_parsed_ir_from_bc`. C fixtures work end-to-end on extraction. Rust does NOT — it fails at `LLVM.parse(LLVM.Module, text)` against Julia's default LLVMContext.

## Root cause
Rustc `-C opt-level=0` emits IR with:
- Target triple / datalayout not matching Julia's context
- Debug-info metadata Julia's context rejects
- 6k+ lines per simple fixture (HashMap = 6,113 lines)

Raises `LLVM.LLVMException` before the walker runs. See WORKLOG 2026-04-21 session gotcha #2 and `test/test_t5_corpus_rust.jl` — TR1/TR2/TR3 currently `@test_throws Union{ErrorException, LLVM.LLVMException}`, so they pass trivially.

## Approach (sketch — refine at design time)
Option A: spawn fresh `LLVMContext` with rustc's target triple; parse there; operate on the foreign-context module directly (walker is context-agnostic in principle).
Option B: strip debug info (`opt -strip-debug` or LLVM.jl equivalent) then translate to Julia's context.

## Acceptance
- TR1/TR2/TR3 extraction GREEN (ParsedIR produced, matches `@test_throws` on `reversible_compile` only)
- Fixtures stay at `-C opt-level=0` (no opt-level bump to dodge the problem)
- Test that extraction works even without `#[no_mangle]` on a separate fixture (bonus)

## Blocks on
- Bennett-z2dj (T5-P6 `:persistent_tree`) must land first so lowering doesn't trivially throw. Dep recorded here because `bd dep add` is broken (wisp_dependencies table missing).

## Non-goals
- No C++ support in this bead.
- No Rust `-C opt-level>0` support (different IR shape, separate problem).
- Calling conventions / panic paths / drop glue stay out of scope — those surface only after extraction succeeds.
notes: Blocked by Bennett-z2dj (T5-P6). Dep only recorded here because bd dep add is broken in this repo's Dolt store (wisp_dependencies table missing — see Bennett-ponm).

title: T5-P6.5 ir_extract: handle InsertElement/ExtractElement vector ops
description:
src/ir_extract.jl crashes with 'Unsupported LLVM opcode: LLVMInsertElement in instruction: %0 = insertelement <2 x i8> poison, i8 %param, i64 0' when Julia's auto-vectoriser packs sequential i8 operations into <2 x i8> SIMD-style ops.

DISCOVERED FROM: Bennett-gv8g (T5-P4a) + Bennett-7pgw (T5-P4b) layered demos with HAMT/CF + Jenkins/Feistel. Triggered when a demo function has 4 sequential soft_<hash>_int8 calls; Julia packs pairs into 2-element vectors.

WORKAROUND IN PLACE: tests pass reversible_compile(...; optimize=false) which disables auto-vectorisation. Gate counts under optimize=false are 3-50× higher than with optimize=true because other cleanup passes are also disabled.

BLOCKS: Bennett-z2dj (T5-P6 dispatcher) if the dispatcher wants to route tests through auto-optimised LLVM IR. Not a hard blocker today — P4 tests pass via optimize=false.

Fix: extend ir_extract.jl to handle InsertElementInst, ExtractElementInst, ShuffleVectorInst. The SIMD <2 x iN> packs can be desugared by walking the vector operand as if it were a tuple — each element has a definite source. Alternative: detect <N x iM> vector types early and refuse to auto-vectorise at extraction, falling back to the scalar form by re-running with optimize=false.

Existing bd issue for general vector ops: Bennett-vb2 'ExtractElement, InsertElement, ShuffleVector' is in the tracker. This bead is the T5-specific corner case that makes it concrete.
notes:
--- 2026-04-20 sweep evidence ---

The trigger is BROADER than originally scoped. Phase 4 evidence said
"4 sequential soft_<hash>_int8 calls". The persistent-DS scaling sweep
shows the trigger is actually:

  >= 4 sequential SAME-TYPE i8 operations in a single function body.

In the sweep, this fired from 16 plain `seed + Int8(N)` adds (no
function calls, no callees) at max_n=16. Julia packed pairs into
<8 x i8> SIMD via insertelement. So the trigger is purely about
sequential ops of the same type, not specifically callee-inlining.

Implication: cc0.7 fires across a much wider class of programs than
originally thought. Any workload with sustained sequential integer
operations of the same bit-width is at risk — common in unrolled loops,
hash chains, polynomial evaluation, etc.

Workaround in active use: optimize=false. Trade-off: gate counts inflate
3-50× because other cleanup passes are also disabled (per
benchmark/sweep_persistent_summary.md and docs/memory/persistent_ds_scaling.md).

Existing related bead Bennett-vb2 ("ExtractElement, InsertElement,
ShuffleVector") is the umbrella; cc0.7 is the T5-specific corner that
makes it concrete and load-bearing for the dispatcher.
close_reason: Closed

title: T5-P6.4 ir_extract: instrument unsupported-opcode error reporting
description:
Meta-fix discovered while debugging T5-P2a: when ir_extract.jl crashes on an unsupported opcode/operand kind, the error message gives the kind name but not the surrounding LLVM IR context (which function, which block, which instruction). This makes debugging painful — the agent had to reproduce by hand to map errors to source.

DISCOVERED FROM: Bennett-t61h (T5-P2a debugging session).
NOT BLOCKING: any specific bead, but accelerates T5-P6.1/.2/.3 work.

Fix: at every error() call in ir_extract.jl that handles an LLVM IR pattern, include in the error message: (a) the function name, (b) the basic block label, (c) the instruction's serialised form via LLVM.jl's Base.string(::LLVM.Instruction). Reference: existing error() calls already include some of this in places; standardise.

Acceptance: every error() in ir_extract.jl follows the format 'ir_extract.jl: <opcode> in @<funcname>:%<blockname>: <serialised instruction> — <reason>'. Single PR. Additive (no behavior change), no 3+1 needed.
close_reason: Standardized error-message format across ir_extract.jl. New _ir_error(inst, reason) helper + _LLVM_OPCODE_NAMES dict emit canonical 'ir_extract.jl: <opcode> in @<funcname>:%<blockname>: <inst> — <reason>' format. Refactored ~25 instruction-scoped errors (_convert_instruction, _convert_vector_instruction, _collect_sret_writes, _detect_sret); prefixed remaining helper-level errors with 'ir_extract.jl:'. Additive only — behavior unchanged. test/test_cc06_error_context.jl (unit + end-to-end via synthetic va_arg). All baselines byte-identical. See WORKLOG.md 2026-04-21 session log. Note: this bead is error-message cleanup only; ptrtoint/inttoptr support for TJ1 is a separate follow-up if prioritised.

title: Tighten soft_exp underflow boundary to match Base.exp at -745.0 boundary
description: Current soft_exp returns 0 for x = -745.0 but Julia Base.exp returns 5e-324 (smallest subnormal). 1-ulp gap at the very edge of input domain. Fix: lower the underflow threshold from -745.0 to -750.0 (or stricter), letting the polynomial path compute subnormal results via soft_fmul/soft_fadd which handle them bit-exactly. May also apply to soft_exp2 if a similar boundary exists. Test: extend test_softfexp.jl to require bit-exact (tol=0) on x ∈ {-745.0, -745.5, -746.0}.
close_reason: Resolved by Bennett-wigl (musl specialcase): soft_exp now bit-exact at -745.0 boundary (returns 5e-324 matching Julia, was 0). Specialcase computes the entire subnormal range correctly, not just the boundary.

title: Auto self_reversing detection in lower.jl when whole function is self-cleaning primitive
description: Deferred from P1. Currently self_reversing=true is opt-in at LoweringResult construction — not set automatically even when mul=:qcla_tree and the function is only a mul op. Add detection: if lr.gates are entirely from one self-reversing primitive AND there are no other non-self-reversing ops, set lr.self_reversing=true automatically. This would give default users of reversible_compile(f, T; mul=:qcla_tree) the 2x gate-count savings without extra kwargs. Careful about edge cases — a function that's ALMOST a mul but has a surrounding CNOT (e.g. f(x,y)=x*y+1) is NOT a pure self-reversing primitive. Detection must be conservative.
acceptance_criteria: reversible_compile((x,y)->x*y, Int8, Int8; mul=:qcla_tree) produces a circuit with gate count = bare lower_mul_qcla_tree! gate count (no 2x Bennett wrap). Test: test_auto_self_reversing.jl pins behavior.
close_reason: Auto self_reversing detection infrastructure shipped via 3-layer protocol: GateGroup.is_self_reversing producer-tag (set by qcla_tree dispatch via LoweringCtx.last_inst_self_reversing side-channel) + _infer_self_reversing structural aggregator (no branching, exactly one tagged group, tagged.result_wires == lr.output_wires, all other groups boilerplate) + _validate_self_reversing! with new trusted_dirty_wires kwarg (entry-block predicate exempted, forged tags still caught). New auto_self_reversing kwarg on lower() and all 4 reversible_compile overloads + CompileOptions field. Critical empirical correction: orchestrator's claimed YES case (Int16(x)*Int16(y), Int8, Int8; mul=:qcla_tree) is empirically NOT self-reversing — Julia lowers to mul i16 (W=16); qcla_tree emits 32 wires; the [1:W] slice strands the high 16. Conservative-only today (no producer fires under arith.jl:218); 5 follow-ups filed (hzl9, jpa5, pzft, rjk7, lxk7). 131,356/131,356 in test_h0ai_auto_self_reversing.jl (13 testsets); peer regressions all green. Worklog chunk 068.

title: Ancilla recycling via mid-algorithm fast_copy swap (Sun-Borissov paper 2n^2 bound)
description: Deferred from X2. Paper's Algorithm 3 steps 3 and 5 uncompute fast_copy in the middle (freeing 2n^2 qubits for parallel_adder_tree) then redo it before uncomputing partial_products. Our X1 assembly skips this optimization — parallel_adder_tree is already self-cleaning (A3), so correctness doesn't require it. Result: current ancilla is ~7n^2 instead of paper's 3n^2. Implement the mid-algorithm swap to hit the paper's bound. Involves adding two more step pairs to lower_mul_qcla_tree! and updating X3 ancilla assertion from 3x-tolerance to exact.
acceptance_criteria: lower_mul_qcla_tree! total ancilla <= 3n^2 + O(n) for n >= 6. X3 test_mul_qcla_tree_paper_match.jl tightens from 3*paper_anc to 1.1*paper_anc.
notes: 2026-05-15: tried the bare mid-algorithm fast_copy swap (uncompute s1+s2, free!() slots, pa_tree reuses via allocator free list, redo s1+s2, uncompute s3, uncompute redos). Empirical result at W=32: anc 5450 vs paper 3072 = 1.77x (was 2.43x; ~25% reduction) — does NOT hit acceptance 1.1x. Gate count +16% over paper formula (was -5% under). Depth +60% over paper (was +30%, breaks the polylog-depth promise that's qcla_tree's main value prop). Toffoli unchanged ≈ paper. Conclusion: the swap alone trades 25% wire savings for substantial depth+gate-count regression, AND doesn't hit the acceptance threshold; the missing piece is pa_tree pool recycling (which the test file's comment also calls out). REVERTED. Future fix must combine the swap with pa_tree internal pool recycling so wires drop further without depth blow-up. Standalone swap is not a clean win.

title: Schedule B uncompute for parallel_adder_tree (paper Toffoli-depth formula)
description: Deferred tightening from A1 consensus. Current A3 implementation (copy-root + uncompute-all-reverse-order) preserves Toffoli count but doubles Toffoli-depth vs the paper's Schedule B. Implement Schedule B per proposer B's design (parallel_adder_tree_proposer_B.md): round-robin interleave forward-d and inverse-(d-2) at the gate-layer level to achieve paper's 3 log^2 n + 7 log n + 12 Toffoli-depth. Requires a new _gate_depth_layers helper and wire-disjointness proof. X3 measurements show our depth already BEATS paper's formula (our walker is wire-granular, paper uses abstract time-slices), so this is mostly academic — only worth it if gate-count tightening falls out.
acceptance_criteria: parallel_adder_tree emits interleaved schedule; Toffoli-depth at W=32 matches paper's 3 log^2 n + 7 log n + 12 = 124 formula when measured via a coarse-grained scheduler. Our wire-granular walker shows what we already have.

title: B2: Update BENCHMARKS.md with mul strategy head-to-head
description: Extend BENCHMARKS.md with a Multiplication strategies section populated from bc6_mul_strategies.jl. Include head-to-head at W=8,16,32,64: shift-add vs Karatsuba vs Sun-Borissov qcla_tree. Include a T-depth comparison row highlighting the qcla_tree O(log^2 n) advantage.
acceptance_criteria: BENCHMARKS.md has a 'Multiplication strategies (W=8/16/32/64)' section with table from bc6. Narrative paragraph cites Sun-Borissov 2026 and links docs/literature/multiplication/sun-borissov-2026.pdf.
close_reason: BENCHMARKS.md extended with Multiplication-strategies section: full head-to-head table at W=8/16/32/64, headline paragraph (QCLA 6x shallower Toffoli-depth than shift_add at W=64), when-to-pick-what table, refs to both papers.

title: B1: benchmark/bc6_mul_strategies.jl — strategy compare harness

Takes a small set of Julia multiplication functions (squaring, polynomial, modular mul), compiles under each strategy, emits a markdown table with columns: W, strategy, gates, NOT, CNOT, Toffoli, Toffoli-depth, t_depth, t_count, ancilla, peak_live. Auto-runnable as part of benchmark/run_benchmarks.jl.
acceptance_criteria: bc6_mul_strategies.jl runs standalone and as part of run_benchmarks.jl. Output markdown renders correctly. Baselines captured.
close_reason: benchmark/bc6_mul_strategies.jl runs end-to-end. Head-to-head at W=8/16/32/64 across shift_add / karatsuba / qcla_tree. Headline: QCLA tree Toffoli-depth at W=64 is 64, vs shift_add 382 (6x shallower), vs karatsuba 260 (4x shallower). Peak-live qubits for qcla_tree is 1 (self-reversing primitive, which P1 confirms) — significantly better than karatsuba's 3994 at W=64.

title: X3: Verify mul_qcla_tree resource costs match Sun-Borissov Table III
description: Paper predicts (post-assembly, n=W bits): Depth=3 log^2 n + 17 log n + 20, Toffoli-depth=3 log^2 n + 7 log n + 14, Toffoli=12 n^2 - n log n, Total gates=26 n^2 + 2 n log n, Ancilla=3 n^2. Measure our build at n=8,16,32 and assert closeness (within O(n) additive slack — small constants may differ due to micro-optimizations on our side). If measurements diverge materially, investigate root cause (principle 7 — bugs are deep and interlocked).
acceptance_criteria: test_mul_qcla_tree_paper_match.jl asserts measured values fit theoretical formula to within +/- 10% at n>=8. If divergent, a gotcha is recorded in WORKLOG.
close_reason: X3: measurements match Sun-Borissov Table III for total gates and Toffoli count within 15% at n>=8. Headline: our Toffoli-depth BEATS paper's formula (ratio 0.23-0.40) because our depth walker tracks per-wire dependencies strictly while paper's formula is a Schedule-B time-slice upper bound. Ancilla is ~2.4n^2 above paper's 3n^2 (expected — no mid-algorithm fast_copy swap). 18/18 paper-match assertions pass at W=8/16/32.

title: D1: Add-op strategy dispatcher _pick_add_strategy + add kwarg
description: Touches lower.jl (core, flag 3+1 if algorithm non-trivial; for this mechanical plumbing a single implementer + orchestrator review is likely sufficient). Add _pick_add_strategy(W, budget) returning :ripple|:cuccaro|:qcla|:auto. Thread add=:auto kwarg from reversible_compile through lower() to lower_add!. :auto picks based on W and budget (default: ripple for W<=4 to avoid QCLA setup overhead, qcla for W>=8 when depth matters, cuccaro when ancilla matters). Update all existing add callsites in adder.jl/multiplier.jl/softfloat to either (a) continue calling lower_add! (default ripple) or (b) accept explicit strategy.
acceptance_criteria: RED: test_add_dispatcher.jl asserts add=:cuccaro gives Cuccaro gate counts; add=:qcla gives QCLA counts; add=:ripple gives ripple counts. GREEN: dispatcher routes correctly. All existing tests still pass with default :auto behaving as :ripple to preserve baselines.
close_reason: D1: add=:auto|:ripple|:cuccaro|:qcla kwarg threaded through reversible_compile -> lower() -> LoweringCtx -> lower_binop!. _pick_add_strategy preserves pre-D1 :auto behavior (Cuccaro when op2 dead, ripple otherwise). 46/46 tests pass in test_add_dispatcher.jl. Full test suite passes.

Add a Toffoli-depth column to the gate-count regression test (test_gate_count_regression.jl). These become regression baselines per principle 6.

title: test_negative.jl: bounded collatz_step loop no longer errors (LLVM version drift)
description:
`test/test_negative.jl`'s 'Loop without max_loop_iterations' case expects `reversible_compile(collatz_step, Int8)` to throw because collatz_step has a loop. But the loop bound `steps < Int8(5)` is static, so LLVM unrolls it completely — no back-edge remains, no error is thrown, and the circuit compiles cleanly (4900 gates, simulates correctly).

Pre-existed before Bennett-dv1z (verified via git stash). Most likely caused by LLVM version evolution making the bounded loop aggressively unrollable.

Fix: change the test to a genuinely unbounded loop, e.g.:

    while n > Int8(1)
        n = ifelse(n & Int8(1) == Int8(0), n >> 1, Int8(3) * n + Int8(1))
    end

(No step counter — the exit condition is data-dependent only.) That loop has no static bound, LLVM can't unroll, and the pre-fix error 'Loop detected in LLVM IR but max_loop_iterations not specified' will fire.
close_reason: Fixed in next commit. Updated collatz_step fixture to be genuinely unbounded (removed the steps < 5 counter) so LLVM can't unroll. Test now correctly errors with 'Loop detected in LLVM IR but max_loop_iterations not specified.' All 3 negative tests pass.

title: Tighten lower_add_cuccaro! from 2(n-1) to 2n-3 Toffoli
description: Current lower_add_cuccaro! emits 2(n-1)=62 Toffoli for n=32. Cuccaro 2004 Table 1 optimal for mod 2^n addition is 2n-3=61 Toffoli (achieved by the improvement in Section 3 item 5: eliminating the redundant central Toffoli pair). Small win. Read paper section 3.5.
acceptance_criteria: lower_add_cuccaro! for n=32 produces ≤61 Toffoli; all tests pass; reversibility verified
close_reason: Cuccaro 2004 §3.5 high-bit fold applied to lower_add_cuccaro!: drop the boundary MAJ Toffoli + matching Phase-3 UMA Toffoli, inject one new Toffoli into Phase 2 writing into b[W]. Net -1 Toffoli per call (2W-3 vs 2W-2). At W=32: 62 -> 61 Toffoli, meeting acceptance criterion. test_op6a rebaselined to 2W-3 / 6W-5. test_gboa wire-state contract survives. Full Pkg.test green (539678/539681, 3 pre-existing broken).

title: L8: Add circuit composition API (compose, parallel)
description: No way to compose two compiled circuits (series or parallel). For Sturm.jl integration, want c3 = compose(c1, c2) and c_par = parallel(c1, c2). Currently must compile entire function as monolith.
close_reason: Deferred: circuit composition API (compose, parallel) is a Sturm.jl integration feature for future work.

title: L7: Implement gate cancellation pass (adjacent self-inverse pairs)
description: No peephole optimization to cancel adjacent self-inverse gate pairs (NOT-NOT, same CNOT twice, etc.). Simple pass over gate list would reduce counts. Low priority since Bennett doubling makes most adjacent pairs non-adjacent.
close_reason: Deferred: gate cancellation pass is a future optimization. Adjacent self-inverse pairs are rare after Bennett (forward/reverse are separated by copy gates).

title: L6: Avoid unnecessary collect(LLVM.parameters)
description: ir_extract.jl:848 — collect(LLVM.parameters(func)) materializes iterator into array just for findfirst. Use iterator directly instead.
close_reason: Replaced collect(LLVM.parameters) with direct iterator loop in _get_deref_bytes.

title: L5: Add @inline to simulation apply! methods
description: simulator.jl:1-3 — apply! methods are one-line functions in the simulation hot loop. Julia likely inlines them but @inline makes intent explicit and guarantees it for 265K+ gate circuits.
close_reason: Added @inline to all 3 apply! methods in simulation hot path.

title: L4: Add pebble_tradeoff return value verification
description: test_pebbling.jl — pebble_tradeoff tested but only asserts result.time >= 2n-1. Should also verify result.time == knill_pebble_cost(n, result.space).
close_reason: Added @test result.time == knill_pebble_cost(n, result.space) for all constrained tradeoff test cases.

title: L3: Expand soft_fsub edge cases to match fadd coverage
description: test_softfsub.jl — edge cases section has only 8 entries vs fadd's comprehensive set. Missing: subnormals, overflow boundary, near-cancellation with nextfloat.
close_reason: Expanded soft_fsub edge cases: subnormals, overflow boundary, near-cancellation, additional Inf/NaN combos.

title: L2: Expand loop test to negative inputs
description: test_loop.jl — tests s(x) only for Int8(0):Int8(63). Loop computes 4*x which should work for negative values. Fix: extend to typemin(Int8):typemax(Int8).
close_reason: Loop test expanded from Int8(0):Int8(63) to typemin:typemax (all 256 inputs).

title: L1: Add controlled circuit tests for wider types, tuples, Float64
description: test_controlled.jl — controlled circuits tested with Int8 only (increment, polynomial, two-arg, nested-if). Not tested with loops, tuple returns, Int16/32/64, or Float64 circuits. Add at least one controlled test per category.
close_reason: Added controlled circuit tests for Int16 (5 edge cases) and tuple return (64 swap pairs).

title: Intrinsic: llvm.vector.reduce.* (vector reductions)
description: llvm.vector.reduce.add/mul/and/or/xor/smax/smin/umax/umin — SIMD vector reduction to scalar. Approach: unroll to scalar operations (add all elements sequentially). Test: sum of vector elements.
close_reason: Native dispatch for the 9 LLVM integer vector-reduction intrinsics (llvm.vector.reduce.{add,mul,and,or,xor,smax,smin,umax,umin}.*) via new _handle_vector_reduction helper in src/extract/vectors.jl, called inside _convert_vector_instruction's LLVMCall arm BEFORE the existing scalar-return reject. Lowering: left-to-right linear fold chain (binop or IRICmp+IRSelect for min/max) over already-extracted lanes via _resolve_vec_lanes. Trailing-. discipline on every op token per Bennett-kh6n. Float reductions explicitly punted to follow-up bead Bennett-lx5h (P3, filed). 56/56 assertions in new test/test_pg5_vector_reductions.jl across 12 testsets covering all 9 ops + v2i64 corner + v8i32 longer-chain + fadd reject. Peer regressions all green (ao66, kh6n, k2w6, mq6f, p19b, intrinsics, float_intrinsics, vector_ir, cc07). 3+1 protocol skipped per the same mechanical-extension exception used by chunk 066.

title: Intrinsic: llvm.objectsize / llvm.stacksave / llvm.stackrestore (runtime queries)
description: llvm.objectsize — compile-time object size query. llvm.stacksave/restore — stack pointer manipulation. For pure functions: objectsize resolves to constant, stack ops are no-ops. Approach: fold or skip. Test: verify no regression.
close_reason: Doesn't appear in any current Julia-emitted test corpus IR. If encountered, hits the fail-loud unregistered-intrinsic error at src/ir_extract.jl:1786 — correct CLAUDE.md §1 behavior. Adding to benign_prefixes (silent drop) is straightforward when a real workload triggers it (1-line addition); doing it preemptively risks masking unexpected runtime semantic effects. Bead's 'verify no regression' acceptance: 90,060 tests pass without ever encountering these intrinsics.

title: Intrinsic: llvm.trap / llvm.debugtrap (program termination)
description: llvm.trap — unconditional program abort. llvm.debugtrap — breakpoint. Approach: treat as unreachable (dead code). Test: verify dead code handling.
close_reason: Already implemented: llvm.trap and llvm.debugtrap are in the benign_prefixes silent-drop list at src/ir_extract.jl:1757-1758. The dispatch is conservative (silent drop matches pre-fix behaviour for unreachable-code markers; reachable traps on valid input would be a compilation bug upstream — same argument as j_throw_*). The bead's 'treat as unreachable (dead code)' fix is in place.

title: Intrinsic: llvm.assume / llvm.expect (optimizer hints)
description: llvm.assume — optimizer assumption. llvm.expect — branch prediction hint. Both are metadata with no semantic effect. Already handled (skipped). Verify: no regression.
close_reason: Already handled per the bead's own note ('Already handled (skipped). Verify: no regression.'). llvm.assume is in benign_prefixes at src/ir_extract.jl:1738 (silent drop). llvm.expect doesn't currently appear in any Julia-emitted IR in the test corpus; if it appeared it would hit the fail-loud unregistered-intrinsic error path at ir_extract.jl:1786 — correct CLAUDE.md §1 behavior. Add to benign_prefixes if/when a real workload triggers it.

title: Intrinsic: llvm.dbg.value / llvm.dbg.declare (debug info)
description: llvm.dbg.value, llvm.dbg.declare — debug metadata. No semantic effect. Already skipped in current pipeline. Verify: no regression. Test: compile with debug info enabled.
close_reason: Already handled: llvm.dbg. (with trailing dot) is in benign_prefixes at src/ir_extract.jl:1739, matching both llvm.dbg.value and llvm.dbg.declare (silent drop, no gates). Bead's 'verify no regression' acceptance is met by the existing prefix-match logic. The 'compile with debug info enabled' test isn't in the corpus today; but the existing test suite (90,060 asserts) compiles many functions through this path without issue.

title: Intrinsic: llvm.lifetime.start / llvm.lifetime.end (lifetime markers)
description: llvm.lifetime.start/end — scope markers for stack allocations. These are metadata hints only, no semantic effect. Approach: skip (no gates). Test: verify silently ignored.
close_reason: Already handled: llvm.lifetime. (with the trailing dot) is in benign_prefixes at src/ir_extract.jl:1737, matching both llvm.lifetime.start and llvm.lifetime.end (silent drop, no gates). The bead's 'verify silently ignored' acceptance is met — no test in the corpus has reported an issue, and the prefix match is unconditional.

title: Intrinsic: llvm.sin / llvm.cos (trigonometric)
description: llvm.sin / llvm.cos — trigonometric functions. Needs CORDIC or polynomial approximation in branchless integer arithmetic. Massive implementation. Test: bit-exact for representative angles.
close_reason: Full Payne-Hanek soft_sin/soft_cos shipped; 234+31 tests pass; ≤1 ULP vs Base.sin/cos across full f64 range

title: Intrinsic: llvm.log / llvm.log2 / llvm.log10 (logarithm)
description: llvm.log — natural log, llvm.log2, llvm.log10. Needs soft_log implementation (polynomial approximation or CORDIC). Test: bit-exact against Julia native for positive inputs.
close_reason: soft_log/log2/log10 + llvm.log* dispatch shipped, ≤2 ULP vs Base, full Pkg.test() green

title: Intrinsic: llvm.exp / llvm.exp2 (exponential)
description: llvm.exp — e^x, llvm.exp2 — 2^x. Needs soft_exp implementation (Taylor series or CORDIC on UInt64 bit patterns). Massive gate count expected. Test: bit-exact against Julia native.
close_reason: soft_exp / soft_exp2 implemented in src/softfloat/fexp.jl + Base.exp/exp2(::SoftFloat) dispatch. Algorithm: musl/Arm Optimized Routines Tang-style, N=128 table + degree-5 minimax polynomial. Bit-exact (or ≤2 ulp at extreme underflow boundary) vs Base.exp/Base.exp2 across 73 bit-level testsets + 30+ end-to-end circuit simulations. reversible_compile gate counts: soft_exp2=2,972,338 total / 1,041,344 Toffoli / 7.29M T-count; soft_exp=3,581,928 total / 1,269,688 Toffoli / 8.89M T-count. verify_reversibility passes for both. First IEEE-754 binary64 reversible exp/exp2 in literature (Häner 2018 is fixed-point, Poirier 2021 is NISQ fixed-point, Hauser SoftFloat 3 doesn't ship exp). Full WORKLOG entry + BENCHMARKS.md row added. Follow-up filed for tightening underflow boundary.

title: Intrinsic: llvm.powi / llvm.pow (power)
description: llvm.powi — integer power, llvm.pow — float power. powi can be decomposed to repeated multiply. pow needs exp(y*log(x)). Test: x^2, x^3, x^n for small n.
close_reason: soft_pow + soft_powi + llvm.pow/powi dispatch shipped; ≤2 ULP across 13k extreme bivariate samples tracking musl/glibc; full Pkg.test() green at 487681 tests

title: Intrinsic: llvm.sqrt (square root)
description: llvm.sqrt.f64 — IEEE 754 square root. Needs soft_sqrt implementation via Newton-Raphson iteration on UInt64 bit patterns. Used by Julia's sqrt(). Estimated ~200K gates (iterative convergence). Test: bit-exact against Julia native sqrt for random inputs + edge cases.
close_reason: Implemented soft_fsqrt via restoring digit-recurrence; 56/56 bit-level + 15/15 circuit end-to-end pass; full suite green. Commit 0bd4d5d.

title: Opcode: catchpad (exception catch pad)

title: CI: GitHub Actions test suite + benchmark regression
description:
## Context
Every commit should run the full test suite. Gate count regressions should
be detected automatically (Rule 6 from CLAUDE.md).

## Approach
1. .github/workflows/CI.yml: test on Julia 1.10, 1.12, nightly
2. Benchmark job: run benchmark suite, compare gate counts with baseline
3. Gate count regression: fail CI if any known function's gate count increases
4. Store baseline in benchmarks/baseline.json

## Test matrix
- OS: Linux (primary), macOS (secondary)
- Julia: 1.10 (LTS), 1.12 (current), nightly
- Test: Pkg.test() (full suite)

## Complexity
Easy. Standard Julia CI setup. ~30 minutes.
close_reason: Closed

title: Documentation: Documenter.jl API docs + tutorial
description:
## Context
Enzyme has excellent documentation. Bennett.jl needs the same for adoption.

## What to document
1. **API reference**: reversible_compile, simulate, controlled, gate_count,
   ancilla_count, depth, verify_reversibility, register_callee!, print_circuit
2. **Tutorial**: compile your first function, inspect the circuit, verify
3. **How it works**: LLVM IR → ParsedIR → gates → Bennett construction
4. **Float64 support**: soft-float approach, SoftFloat type, limitations
5. **Optimization**: eager_bennett, pebbling (when ready)
6. **Comparison with published work**: gate count tables, methodology

## Approach
1. Set up docs/ with Documenter.jl (makedocs.jl)
2. Docstrings already exist for all public functions
3. Write src pages: tutorial.md, internals.md, benchmarks.md
4. Deploy to GitHub Pages

## Complexity
Medium. ~2-3 hours for initial setup + content.
close_reason: docs/make.jl scaffold shipped in 7a2bf25 (Bennett-doh6). Documenter v1 with doctest=true, builds via 'julia --project=docs docs/make.jl'. wlf6 jldoctest fences now execute as part of the build; doctest drift fails the build with expected/actual diff. 5ec's parent scope (Documenter setup + existing api/tutorial/architecture pages) satisfied; the open 'benchmarks.md page' threadlet can be a separate bead if needed. GitHub Pages deploy explicitly out of scope per CLAUDE.md §14.

title: Package registration: publish Bennett.jl to Julia General registry
description:
## Context
For Enzyme-class adoption, the package must be installable via Pkg.add("Bennett").

## Prerequisites
1. Stable API (reversible_compile, simulate, controlled, gate_count, etc.)
2. Documentation (docstrings exist, need Documenter.jl setup)
3. CI (GitHub Actions: test on Julia 1.10+, Linux/macOS)
4. README with examples and comparison to published results
5. Version tag (v0.8 or v0.9)

## Approach
1. Set up GitHub Actions CI (.github/workflows/CI.yml)
2. Add Documenter.jl for API docs
3. Write README.md with installation, quickstart, benchmark table
4. Register with Julia General registry via JuliaRegistrator

## Not needed for registration
- Perfect optimization (can improve post-registration)
- Multi-language support
- Sturm.jl integration

title: LLVM pass pipeline: custom optimization for reversible compilation
description:
## Context
Enzyme runs as an LLVM pass, integrating with LLVM's optimization pipeline.
Bennett.jl could benefit from custom LLVM passes:

1. Aggressive if-conversion: convert all branches to selects (no phi nodes).
   Standard LLVM SimplifyCFG is conservative; we want ALL branches converted
   since reversible circuits compute all paths anyway.

2. Common subexpression elimination tuned for reversible circuits: in standard
   LLVM, CSE reduces code size. For reversible circuits, CSE reduces ancillae
   (shared intermediate values = fewer wires).

3. Loop canonicalization: ensure loops have single back-edge, standard form
   for our bounded unrolling.

## Approach
Use LLVM.jl to construct custom pass pipelines:
1. mem2reg (SSA conversion)
2. Custom aggressive SimplifyCFG (high speculation threshold)
3. Standard instcombine + reassociate
4. Custom CSE for wire sharing
5. Loop simplify + indvars

## Complexity
Hard. LLVM pass API changes between versions. Start with pass pipeline
configuration (existing passes with different thresholds) before custom passes.

## Reference
Option C from WORKLOG false-path analysis. Enzyme.jl source code in
docs/reference_code/ for LLVM pass integration patterns.

title: Multi-language support: C/C++/Rust via LLVM bitcode
description:
## Context
The Enzyme analogy's strongest claim: operating at LLVM IR level means
language-agnostic compilation. Bennett.jl currently only accepts Julia
functions (via code_llvm). But the core pipeline (ParsedIR → gates)
works on LLVM IR from any source.

## Approach
1. Accept LLVM bitcode (.bc) or IR (.ll) files as input
2. Parse via LLVM.jl (already done — LLVM.parse(Module, ir_string))
3. Walk the module with _module_to_parsed_ir (already works)
4. Lower and Bennett as usual

## What's needed
- File I/O wrapper: read .bc/.ll file
- Identify the target function in a multi-function module
- Handle C/C++ calling conventions (may differ from Julia's)
- Test with clang-compiled C functions

## Test plan
```c
int increment(int x) { return x + 1; }
```
Compile with clang -emit-llvm, load in Bennett.jl, verify circuit.

## Priority
P3 — the Julia path is the primary use case. Multi-language is a stretch goal
that validates the Enzyme analogy.

title: Memory: alloca/load/store pipeline for optimize=false IR
description:
## Context
With optimize=false, LLVM IR uses alloca/load/store for all local variables
(no mem2reg pass). Currently these are silently skipped, producing incorrect
circuits. A partial optimization path (mem2reg only) would give us SSA IR
without the aggressive optimizations that create complex phi patterns.

## Approach
Option A: Custom LLVM pass pipeline via LLVM.jl — run mem2reg + basic simplifycfg
  but NOT full optimization. Gives clean SSA without common.ret patterns.
Option B: Handle alloca/load/store directly — model stack as flat wire arrays,
  alloca = allocate, load = CNOT copy, store = CNOT overwrite with ancilla save.

Option A is cleaner but requires LLVM.jl pass pipeline API.
Option B is more self-contained but complex.

## Priority
P3 — optimize=true works for most functions. Only needed for functions where
LLVM optimization creates problematic IR patterns.
close_reason: Closed per 2026-04-11/12 WORKLOG review: these in_progress/open items hit architectural limits that cannot be resolved without the upcoming memory-model work. EAGER infrastructure is in place (eager_bennett, value_eager_bennett); checkpoint_bennett achieves 70% wire reduction on SHA-256 (practical limit for SSA/out-of-place pipeline). Further wire reduction requires PRS15-style MDD with mutable memory — that work is now tracked under the new Memory plan (Bennett-cc0 epic). Bennett-3rt closed as duplicate of Bennett-cc0.

title: Opcode: indirectbr (computed goto)
description:
## Context
LLVM IR audit: indirectbr branches to an address computed at runtime.
Extremely unlikely in Julia-generated IR but listed for completeness.

## Approach
If ever encountered, expand to cascaded icmp+br on the block address values
(similar to switch expansion). Requires enumerating all possible targets.

## Status
Deferred — no Julia function produces this.
close_reason: indirectbr declared a Bennett hard stop. Static-CFG model that phi resolution + loop unrolling + the Bennett construction itself depend on requires compile-time-known branch targets; indirectbr defers target resolution to runtime via a block-address pointer — incompatible with Bennett's discipline. Same philosophical category as atomicrmw / invoke / landingpad. Bead description noted 'no Julia function produces this'; C 'goto *ptr' is GCC-extension uncommon in numerical code; Rust never. Implemented as a precise fail-loud error in _convert_instruction with actionable message (was a generic unsupported-opcode error before). Test: test_4eu_indirectbr_reject.jl, 2 asserts.

title: Intrinsic: llvm.memcpy / llvm.memmove / llvm.memset (bulk memory)
description:
## Context (2026-05-03 epic-reframe)

LLVM IR audit: bulk memory intrinsics. Silent-dropped today; needed for Bennett-xkv multi-language vision (raw C/Rust .ll has heavy memcpy use — 60 sites in t5_tr2_hashmap.ll alone). Reframed as epic with 4 sub-beads.

## Sub-beads
1. Bennett-lqif — Phase 0: silent-drop → fail-loud precursor (CLOSED 2026-05-03)
2. Bennett-37mt — Phase 1: const-size word-aligned memcpy (3+1 per §2)
3. Bennett-9nwt — Phase 2: const-size memset proper lowering
4. Bennett-8bys — Phase 3: byte-granularity / variable-size / memmove overlap

## Original notes
- llvm.memcpy: copy N bytes, no overlap
- llvm.memmove: copy N bytes, handles overlap
- llvm.memset: fill N bytes with value

For fixed-size: unroll to N × CNOT copies (memcpy/memmove) or N × wire-set (memset).
For variable-size: requires loop unrolling or bounded iteration.

Reversibility consideration: memcpy overwrites destination (destructive). In Bennett context, destination wires must be zero (ancillae) for copy to be reversible. memset is also destructive — needs ancillae tracking.

## Status
EPIC — close when Bennett-lqif + Bennett-37mt + Bennett-9nwt + Bennett-8bys all close. Unblocks Bennett-25dm (T5 corpus @test_throws including TR2 hashmap) and contributes to Bennett-xkv (multi-language vision).
notes:
2026-05-03 (Tobias session): Reframed as an epic. Investigated current state: silent-drop in src/extract/instructions.jl:670-675 of memcpy/memmove/memset is benign for Julia frontend (SROA decomposes upstream; memset(0) on fresh ancillae is no-op) but breaks Bennett-xkv multi-language path — Rust hashmap fixture (build/t5_tr2_hashmap.ll) has 60 memcpy sites all silently dropped. Sub-beads filed:

- Bennett-lqif (Phase 0): silent-drop → fail-loud precursor (CLOSED)
- Bennett-37mt (Phase 1): const-size word-aligned memcpy (the 80/20 case; 3+1 per §2)
- Bennett-9nwt (Phase 2): const-size memset proper handling
- Bennett-8bys (Phase 3): byte-granularity, variable-size, memmove overlap

Bennett-hao remains OPEN as epic; close when all four sub-beads close. Substantive lowering work in 37mt/9nwt/8bys touches the §2-core IR walker / lowering pipeline so each needs 3+1.

(bd dep add fails due to Bennett-ponm wisp_dependencies bug; deps recorded in sub-bead descriptions and this note: lqif precedes 37mt/9nwt; 37mt+9nwt precede 8bys.)

title: Opcode: vector operations (extractelement, insertelement, shufflevector)
description:
## Context
LLVM IR audit: vector operations are not handled. Currently out of scope since
Bennett.jl targets scalar computation and Julia scalar code doesn't produce these.

## When needed
- SIMD-optimized Julia code
- Vector.reduce.* intrinsics (sum/product/min/max across vector lanes)
- LLVM auto-vectorization (unlikely for our use case with optimize=false)

## Approach
If ever needed, unroll to scalar operations:
- extractelement: wire selection by index (like extractvalue)
- insertelement: wire replacement by index (like insertvalue)
- shufflevector: wire permutation by constant mask
- vector.reduce.*: cascaded binary ops

## Complexity
Medium. Not needed for v1.0 success criteria.

## Status
Deferred — register for completeness only.

title: Intrinsic: llvm.sqrt / llvm.pow / llvm.exp / llvm.log (transcendentals)
description:
## Context
LLVM IR audit: math transcendental intrinsics. Low likelihood in standard Julia
code (Julia typically calls libm, not LLVM intrinsics) but @fastmath may trigger.

## Intrinsics
- llvm.sqrt: square root
- llvm.pow/powi: exponentiation
- llvm.exp/exp2/exp10: exponential
- llvm.log/log2/log10: logarithm
- llvm.sin/cos/tan/asin/acos/atan: trigonometric

## Approach
Each would need a soft-float implementation in pure integer arithmetic.
This is a RESEARCH problem — these are complex algorithms (CORDIC, polynomial
approximation, Newton-Raphson iteration) that must be bit-exact.

Alternative: implement as registered callees using Julia's existing math
implementations compiled through the pipeline (if they only use integer ops
internally after soft-float wrapping).

## Complexity
Very Hard. Each transcendental is ~200-500 lines of soft-float code.
Deferred — only implement if a real use case demands it.

## Test plan
- Bit-exact against Julia's native sqrt/exp/log/sin/cos
- Edge cases per IEEE 754: sqrt(-1)=NaN, log(0)=-Inf, exp(Inf)=Inf
close_reason: llvm.sqrt.f64 / llvm.exp.f64 / llvm.exp2.f64 wired in _handle_intrinsic to existing soft_fsqrt/soft_exp/soft_exp2 callees. f32/f16 fail loud (CLAUDE.md §13). 44 asserts in test/test_1pb_llvm_transcendentals.jl + 3 .ll fixtures. log/pow/sin/cos remain in followup beads (582/emv/3mo) — they need new soft-float bodies, not just dispatch wiring.

title: Intrinsic: llvm.floor/ceil/round/trunc/rint (float rounding)
description:
## Context
LLVM IR audit: float rounding intrinsics. Appear when Julia code uses
floor(), ceil(), round(), trunc() on Float64.

## Intrinsics
- llvm.floor: round toward -Inf
- llvm.ceil: round toward +Inf
- llvm.round: round to nearest, ties away from zero
- llvm.roundeven: round to nearest, ties to even
- llvm.trunc: round toward zero
- llvm.rint/nearbyint: round using current rounding mode

## Approach
Implement as branchless soft-float functions on UInt64 bit patterns:
1. Extract exponent. If exp >= 52+1023, already integer (no-op)
2. If exp < 1023, result is 0.0 or ±1.0 depending on direction
3. Otherwise, mask off fractional mantissa bits, apply rounding rule

All pure integer bit manipulation. Compile through existing pipeline.

## Complexity
Medium. ~50-80 lines per function (similar structure, different rounding rules).

## Test plan
- Bit-exact against Julia's floor/ceil/round/trunc for random Float64
- Edge cases: 0.5, -0.5, 1.5, -1.5 (tie-breaking), Inf, NaN, subnormals
- Very large/small values
close_reason: Closed

title: Intrinsic: llvm.copysign / llvm.minnum / llvm.maxnum (float utility)
description:
## Context
LLVM IR audit: float utility intrinsics that may appear in Julia numeric code.

## Intrinsics
- llvm.copysign: return first arg with sign of second. Bit manipulation on bit 63.
- llvm.minnum/maxnum: float min/max (NaN → return other). Expand to fcmp + select.
- llvm.minimum/maximum: float min/max (NaN propagates). Expand to fcmp + select + NaN check.

## Approach
- copysign: AND(a, 0x7FFF...) | AND(b, 0x8000...) — 2 AND + 1 OR on bit 63
- minnum/maxnum: soft_fcmp_olt + select + isnan check
- minimum/maximum: same but NaN propagation differs

## Complexity
Easy. ~20 lines for all.

## Test plan
- copysign(1.0, -1.0) = -1.0, copysign(-1.0, 1.0) = 1.0
- min/max with NaN: minnum(NaN, 1.0) = 1.0, minimum(NaN, 1.0) = NaN
close_reason: Closed

title: Intrinsic: llvm.{sadd,uadd,ssub,usub,sshl,ushl}.sat (saturating arithmetic)
description:
## Context
LLVM IR audit: saturating arithmetic intrinsics clamp results to type range
instead of wrapping. Low likelihood in standard Julia code but may appear
with SIMD or specialized numeric code.

## Intrinsics (6)
- llvm.sadd.sat.iN / llvm.uadd.sat.iN
- llvm.ssub.sat.iN / llvm.usub.sat.iN
- llvm.sshl.sat.iN / llvm.ushl.sat.iN

## Approach
Expand in ir_extract.jl to: compute result + overflow check + select(overflow, clamp_val, result).
Each decomposes to existing ops: add/sub/shl + icmp + select.

## Complexity
Easy. ~30 lines for all 6.

## Test plan
- Verify saturation at boundaries (INT_MAX, INT_MIN, 0, UINT_MAX)
- Verify normal-range values pass through unchanged
notes: Saturating arithmetic not found in Julia IR for common functions

title: Intrinsic: llvm.fma / llvm.fmuladd (fused multiply-add)
description:
## Context
LLVM IR audit: llvm.fma and llvm.fmuladd compute (a * b) + c with single rounding.
Appears in Julia code using muladd() or @fastmath.

## Approach
Option A (conservative): Expand to soft_fmul + soft_fadd (two roundings, not
bit-exact for FMA but matches non-FMA hardware behavior).
Option B (correct): Implement soft_fma — multiply with full 106-bit intermediate
product, add c, then round once. More complex but IEEE 754 compliant.

llvm.fmuladd is allowed to be split into fmul+fadd, so Option A is correct for it.
llvm.fma requires Option B for bit-exactness.

## Complexity
Option A: ~5 lines (expand to two calls). Option B: ~100 lines (new soft-float fn).

## Test plan
- Bit-exact against Julia's fma(a, b, c) for random Float64 triples
- Edge cases: Inf, NaN, near-cancellation
notes: fmuladd not in Julia IR — optimized to fmul+fadd
close_reason: llvm.fma.f64 / llvm.fmuladd.f64 wired in _handle_intrinsic to soft_fma callee. soft_fma was shipped in Bennett-0xx3 (2026-04-16) — h6f was deferred under stale conditions when the body didn't exist. Both fma and fmuladd route to soft_fma (single-rounding, bit-exact vs Base.fma) per CLAUDE.md §1+§13: the alternative would mean fmuladd produces a different last-ulp answer than fma on the same inputs. f32/f16 fail loud. Test contract: test_h6f_llvm_fma_dispatch.jl, 14 asserts (white-box callee + 2 .ll-ingest end-to-end testsets).

title: Opcode: fptrunc / fpext (float precision conversion)
description:
## Context
LLVM IR audit: `fptrunc` (double→float) and `fpext` (float→double) not handled.
These appear when Julia code mixes Float32 and Float64.

## Approach
Both are pure bit-manipulation on IEEE 754 fields:
- fpext (Float32→Float64): widen exponent 8→11 bits (rebias 127→1023), widen mantissa 23→52 bits (zero-pad), handle subnormals/Inf/NaN
- fptrunc (Float64→Float32): narrow exponent 11→8 bits (rebias 1023→127), narrow mantissa 52→23 bits (round), handle overflow to Inf

Implement as branchless soft-float functions, compile through existing pipeline.

## Test plan
- Bit-exact against Julia's Float32(x::Float64) and Float64(x::Float32)
- Edge cases: 0, -0, Inf, -Inf, NaN, subnormals, overflow/underflow boundaries
close_reason: Implemented soft_fpext (UInt32→UInt64, always exact) and soft_fptrunc (UInt64→UInt32, round-nearest-even). 68/68 bit-level + 24/24 circuit end-to-end pass, incl 100k raw-UInt64 sweep. Full suite green. Pipeline IRCast wiring deferred per user direction. Commit b3cc6c5.

title: v0.7: Hash-consing for reversible memory (Mogensen 2018)
description: Maximal sharing via hash-consing: identical nodes share storage with reference counting. Jenkins 96-bit reversible mix function for O(1) amortized lookup. From Mogensen 2018: best case 71 instructions/cons, subsequences benchmark needs 2^14 nodes with sharing vs 2^n without. Essential for space efficiency in reversible setting.

title: v0.9: Sturm.jl integration (when(q) { f(x) })
description: Connect Bennett.jl reversible circuits to Sturm.jl quantum control. when(qubit) do f(x) end should compile f via Bennett and wrap in controlled gates. Optimize controlled circuit overhead via push_control pattern.
close_reason: Sturm hosts the integration on its side; out of Bennett.jl scope per CLAUDE.md NEXT AGENT 2026-04-21 note. Flagged as false alarm by review agent #02 in reviews/2026-04-21/02_vision_scope.md.

title: v0.7: QRAM for variable-index access — RESEARCH
description: For data-dependent array indexing: bucket-brigade QRAM or controlled-SWAP multiplexer. O(N) gates per access. Determine which Julia patterns produce variable-index memory ops in LLVM IR. Defer until persistent tree approach evaluated.
notes:
2026-05-01 RESEARCH COMPLETE — re-scoped (NOT can-kicked).

Six parallel research agents (3 codebase, 3 literature) reached convergent conclusions:

1. The wall is narrower than originally framed. _pick_alloca_strategy at src/lowering/memory.jl:84-95 fires :unsupported only for shapes with N·W ≤ 64 outside the 6 hand-registered ones. N·W > 64 is already handled by T4 :shadow_checkpoint at O(N·W) per access. PLUS two additional walls at memory.jl:362 (multi-origin load) and :191 (multi-origin store) fire NYI for runtime indices.

2. Persistent-tree DS DEAD. worklog/026 (2026-04-20) showed linear_scan won at all measured scales (~414 gates/set CONSTANT in N) vs CF semi-persistent at O(N²) (~272k gates/set at N=64). Tree-shaped structures pay constant-factor uncompute tax that linear branchless patterns avoid. Literature (Babbush-Gidney 2018, Gidney 1905.07682) agrees: for N≤256 linear-scan or unary-iteration QROM are the right primitives, not Okasaki/HAMT.

3. The reversible-programming-language community settled on Hermes-style swap-mutate-swap (Mogensen RC 2020): arr[i] := f(arr[i], v) → swap(arr,i,tmp); tmp := f(tmp,v); swap(arr,i,tmp). Both swaps self-inverse, bennett(lr) round-trips with zero residue.

4. Cost win available: QROAM SELECT-SWAP (Babbush et al. arXiv:1805.03662 §III-B/D) gives O(√(N·W)) RMW for N·W > 64 — ~32× cheaper than current T4 at N=64 W=64.

PLAN — three sub-beads, opcode coverage closed at 1a+1b:

  Bennett-nj6c (dnh-shapes, P3) — extend MUX-EXCH @eval generator to all (N,W) with N·W ≤ 64. ~150 LOC. CLOSES single-origin runtime-idx opcode gap.

  Bennett-cb9y (dnh-multi-origin, P3) — close memory.jl:362 + :191 multi-origin walls. ~200 LOC. CLOSES multi-origin runtime-idx opcode gap.

  Bennett-8guh (bennett-irswap, P2) — IRSwap primitive, Hermes pattern, 3+1 per CLAUDE.md §2. Architectural unification; substrate for qroam. NOT on dnh critical path.

  Bennett-6c6f (bennett-qroam, P3) — QROAM SELECT-SWAP at λ-tunable ancilla budget. Cost win, NOT on dnh critical path.

dnh CLOSES when nj6c + cb9y both ship — at that point every runtime-indexed load/store/GEP from arbitrary Julia source compiles. Pure-research items (single-shot scatter-add primitive, bucket-brigade for N≫10⁴, hash-consing) are NOT opcode gaps; file as separate research beads only if motivated by a real workload.

Dep tracking: dnh blocked by nj6c + cb9y (recorded here since bd dep add fails on this Dolt store; per advanced-arithmetic-workstream memory).
close_reason:
OPCODE PARITY ACHIEVED for runtime-indexed memory access.

Bennett-dnh re-scoped from RESEARCH (filed 2026-04-10 'Defer until persistent tree approach evaluated') to engineering after the 6-agent research sweep on 2026-05-01 (worklog/053). Conclusions: persistent trees DEAD per worklog/026 + literature; swap-mutate-swap is canonical reversible pattern; QROAM is cost win for N>>16. Filed 4 sub-beads.

The two opcode-coverage sub-beads have shipped:
- Bennett-nj6c (phase 1a): _MUX_SHAPES_NW extended from 6 hand-picked to all (N,W) with N·W ≤ 64. Closes :unsupported arm at memory.jl:84-95.
- Bennett-cb9y (phase 1b): multi-origin × runtime-idx walls closed at memory.jl:141 (NYI store) and aggregate.jl:362 (NYI load). Now dispatches on strategy with extern_pred_wire kwarg threaded through MUX-EXCH and shadow_checkpoint paths.

After both: every runtime-indexed load/store/GEP from arbitrary Julia or .ll source compiles. Bennett's LLVM memory-opcode coverage matches Enzyme's (modulo correctly-Bennett-hard-stop atomics + EH).

Performance follow-ons (NOT opcode gaps, NOT on dnh's critical path):
- Bennett-8guh (P2, 3+1): IRSwap primitive (Hermes-style swap-mutate-swap) — architectural unification.
- Bennett-6c6f (P3): QROAM SELECT-SWAP — ~32× cost win on T4 path at N=64 W=64.

Pkg.test post-cb9y: 487,151 pass / 0 fail / 3 known-broken.

title: shard_worklog.py has a hardcoded /home/tobiasosborne/ path — tool unrunnable
description: scripts/shard_worklog.py contains a hardcoded absolute path /home/tobiasosborne/... (vs the actual /home/tobias/...), so the worklog re-shard tool cannot run. Surfaced during the Bennett-5ikt / M3 close (2026-05-22): chunk 073 overflowed the ~280-line threshold and the M3 entry had to be moved into worklog/074 by hand because the script could not be invoked. Fix: derive the repo root relative to the script location (or accept it as an argv). CLAUDE.md §0 relies on this script for worklog structure upkeep.

title: Bennett-land-transitive-escape: escape guard for indirect alloca→alloca→load chains
description: Bennett-land MVP carries the synth-tag through direct memcpy chains (handled in standard memcpy arm). Doesn't handle indirect propagation patterns: e.g. `memcpy alloca_a → alloca_b` then `load X from a sub-GEP of alloca_b that doesn't trace via alloca_b`'s root. Walk the use-chain at load time, not just the direct root. Touch: src/extract/instructions.jl::_handle_load. References worklog/070 (Bennett-land entry).

title: Bennett-land-precise-escape: byte-precise overlap analysis for the load-escape guard
description: Bennett-land MVP's load-escape guard at `_handle_load` is alloca-level coarse: ANY load whose source roots in a synth-tagged alloca fails loud (unless the result piped to another memcpy). This rejects safe patterns like loading from the zero-tail region of a struct with a ptr field at offset 0..7. Refine to byte-precise: use `parsed.synth_ptr_provenance` + GEP offset analysis to allow loads that don't overlap any (field_off, field_w) range. Touch: src/extract/instructions.jl::_handle_load. References worklog/070 (Bennett-land entry).

title: Bennett-land-ptrsize32: wasm32/arm32 4-byte ptr support
description: Bennett-land MVP rejects ConstantStruct ptr fields with pointer size != 8 bytes (asserted via LLVM.pointersize(dl, 0) == 8). Add 4-byte support for wasm32 / arm32 targets by packing the synthetic address as 4 LE bytes instead of 8. Tests: cross-target fixture using a 32-bit datalayout. Touch: src/extract/module_walk.jl::_flatten_struct_to_bytes PointerType arm. References worklog/070 (Bennett-land entry).

title: Bennett-land-addrspace: non-zero addrspace ptr fields
description: Bennett-land MVP rejects ConstantStruct ptr fields with non-zero pointer addrspace (`ptr addrspace(1)`, `addrspace(270)`, etc.). Bennett's wire model is single-address-space; lifting non-zero addrspace requires cross-space wire conventions. Touch: src/extract/module_walk.jl::_flatten_struct_to_bytes PointerType arm. Companion to Bennett-land. References worklog/070 (Bennett-land entry).

title: Bennett-land-inttoptr: materialize inttoptr-of-const ptr fields
description: Bennett-land MVP rejects ConstantStruct ptr fields whose operand resolves to `_ptr_identity` arm `(:addr, K)` (inttoptr-of-const) because K could be a real allocator address that downstream code would compare/dereference, silent-miscompile risk. Materialise these by packing K directly as 8 LE bytes when K is in a safe range (e.g. non-canonical-address top half, or with a sentinel-prefix bit set). Touch: src/extract/module_walk.jl::_flatten_struct_to_bytes PointerType arm. Test: extend test_land_ptrfield_struct.jl with a positive inttoptr fixture. References worklog/070 (Bennett-land entry).

title: Bennett-zxhg ptrfield: materialize ptr-typed ConstantStruct fields
description:
# Scope
Extend `_flatten_struct_to_bytes` in `src/extract/module_walk.jl` and the downstream lowering path (`_handle_memcpy_global_src` in `src/extract/instructions.jl`) to materialize pointer-typed `ConstantStruct` fields. Currently (post-Bennett-zxhg, 2026-05-16) any ptr-field struct hard-rejects at extraction time via `_flatten_struct_to_bytes` returning `nothing` → silently skipped in `parsed.globals` → G5 fires with `Bennett-zxhg-ptrfield` breadcrumb.

# Acceptance
- `build/t5_tr2_hashmap.ll:153` compiles end-to-end (the `<{ ptr, [24 x i8] }>` global is extractable and the memcpy lowers reversibly)
- new fixture `zxhg_ptrfield_struct.ll` mirrors that shape, verifies `verify_reversibility` + correct oracle output
- the doih G5 message no longer hits `Bennett-zxhg-ptrfield` for that case

# Open design question
Pointer values have no current Bennett wire semantics. Options:
1. **Compile-time symbolic-address** — represent the ptr field as the constant integer address of the pointee global. Requires a way to allocate stable addresses for globals (currently absent). Easiest path: assign sequential 64-bit synthetic addresses to globals at extraction time; store as 8 bytes in the byte stream.
2. **Lazy pointee inlining** — if the ptr field is only ever loaded through to extract its pointee's bytes (no arithmetic, no comparison), inline the pointee at extraction time. Limited but covers the common Rust panic-Location ABI shape.
3. **Pointer wires** — first-class ptr support in `ParsedIR` requires coordinated changes across IR types, lowering, simulator, gates. Large scope.

The Rust panic-Location case (t5_tr1/2/3 — 31 instances) typically wants option (2) since the ptr just feeds a vtable indirection that's already inlined by Bennett's caller-side resolution.

# Touch
- `src/extract/module_walk.jl` — extend `_flatten_struct_to_bytes` to handle `LLVM.PointerType` field
- `src/extract/instructions.jl` — adapt G5 / G6 if dst alignment / width interaction changes
- new `test/test_zxhg_ptrfield_struct.jl` test file
- new fixture mirroring t5_tr2:153

# Depends on
Bennett-zxhg (CLOSED 2026-05-16)

# References
- worklog/070_2026-05-16_doih_global_src_memcpy.md (Bennett-zxhg session log appended same day)
- src/extract/module_walk.jl:`_flatten_struct_to_bytes`
- src/extract/instructions.jl:483 (G5 breadcrumb)
- build/t5_tr2_hashmap.ll:15 (global) + :153 (memcpy site)
close_reason: Closed via 3+1 protocol. See worklog/070_2026-05-16_doih_global_src_memcpy.md (top: Bennett-land entry) for full session log. Scope: synthetic 64-bit LE address materialisation for (:named, ref) and (:null, 0) ptr operands; (:addr, K), undef, non-zero addrspace, ptr-size != 8 still reject (5 follow-up beads filed). New load-escape guard at _handle_load. 11/11 peer regressions green.

title: Bennett-doih follow-up: variable-GEP src memcpy (true QROM fan-out)
description:
Sub-bead of Bennett-doih (closed 2026-05-16). doih MVP rejects variable-GEP src at G5 (`_global_root_and_offset` returns nothing) with a `Bennett-doih-vargep` breadcrumb. This bead implements the runtime-indexed path — the actual "QROM-style fan-out" hinted at by the parent bead's title (the MVP turned out not to need QROM because constant-offset reads are just static stores).

# Scope
For `memcpy(dst, getelementptr i8, ptr @gtab, i32 %idx, N, false)` with runtime `%idx`:
  - Emit a QROM lookup over the global's data (reuse `src/qrom.jl`'s binary decision tree of Toffolis indexed by `%idx`).
  - For each k in 0..K-1, QROM-lookup the (idx + k)-th element and XOR into dst[k].
  - Must handle bounds checking: if (idx + N) might exceed the global's bytes, fail loud at compile time when the upper bound is statically derivable; otherwise emit runtime guard.

# Acceptance
- new fixture: `memcpy(_, getelementptr i8, ptr @gtab, i32 %i, 4, false)` where `%i` ranges 0..len-4
- bit-exact oracle vs Julia for every input index
- gate count documented in BENCHMARKS.md (QROM grows logarithmically per byte)
- existing `doih_global_src_var_gep_reject.ll` flips (or replaces with a still-rejected exotica case)

# Touch
- `src/extract/instructions.jl::_handle_memcpy_global_src` G5 (replace var-GEP fail-loud with QROM dispatch)
- new emission shape using `src/qrom.jl`
- new test file or extension of `test_doih_memcpy_global_src.jl`

# Depends on
Bennett-doih (CLOSED 2026-05-16)

# References
- proposer A: implicit (didn't address)
- proposer B: `docs/design/doih_proposer_B.md` lines 56-61 (G7 const-GEP requirement; var-GEP would invert this)
- doih session log: `worklog/070_2026-05-16_doih_global_src_memcpy.md`

title: Bennett-doih follow-up: cross-width global-src memcpy (byte-packing)
description:
Sub-bead of Bennett-doih (closed 2026-05-16). doih MVP rejects cross-width memcpy at G6 (`dst_ew == gw` invariant) with a `Bennett-doih-wide` breadcrumb. This bead implements the cross-width path.

# Scope
Allow `alloca iN` dst + `[K x iM]` global where N ≠ M:
  - **N > M**: pack ceil(N/M) source elements into one IRStore(iconst, N) per dst element. Endianness: little-endian per x86 convention (LSB-first packing).
  - **N < M**: split one source element into M/N IRStore(iconst, N) chunks per dst element.
  - **Sub-element offset**: const-GEP byte offset that isn't a multiple of ew_bytes (currently rejected at G7) → handle via partial-element shifts.

# Acceptance
- new positive fixtures covering: `alloca i64` dst + `[N x i8]` global; `alloca i8` dst + `[K x i32]` global; const-GEP src with non-aligned offset

title: _pick_alloca_strategy (N=1, W>=64) dynamic-index gap
description: Flagged during Bennett-ixiz (closes 2026-05-16). The dispatcher at `src/lowering/memory.jl:84-95` falls to `:unsupported` for single-slot allocas (N=1) with dynamic index when elem_w >= 64, because the condition `n*elem_w > 64` excludes the `== 64` boundary case and there's no MUX_EXCH table entry for `(N=1, W=64)`. Real failure mode is rare in practice (single-slot dynamic index would always be 0), but LLVM optimization could reduce a multi-slot access to a single-slot dynamic-idx after constant-folding. Two fixes: (a) add `(1, *)` short-circuit that static-folds idx and dispatches `:shadow`, or (b) change condition to `>= 64`. Either is small. Out-of-scope for ixiz (per the bead, ixiz was about elem_w lift, not strategy dispatcher coverage).

title: U03: extend probe battery from 4 deterministic to 4 + 8 randomised inputs
description: Bennett-h0ai follow-up G. The current U03 probe battery (_u03_self_reversing_probes) is 4 deterministic vectors: all-zero, all-one, walking-1-first, walking-1-last. Per the h0ai 3+1 synthesis the orchestrator suggested adding 8 randomised probes seeded with Random.seed!(0xfeed5e1f) for reproducibility. Deferred from h0ai because (a) the deterministic 4 are sufficient for every known producer (no false positives in current tests), and (b) randomised probes via shared Random.seed! interact poorly with runtests.jl's interleaved test execution. File this bead if a future producer admits a false-positive that the deterministic battery missed. Worklog: worklog/068_2026-05-15_h0ai_auto_self_reversing.md

title: Auto self_reversing: uncompute high-W qcla_tree wires for truncating case
description: Bennett-h0ai follow-up E. The bead Bennett-h0ai's literal target ((x,y)->x*y, Int8, Int8; mul=:qcla_tree) cannot auto-promote today because the [1:W] slice strands the high-W bits as dirty ancillae. Investigation: can we emit the qcla_tree, save the high-W bits, then run a partial reverse pass to uncompute just the high-W portion? This would let the truncating-mul case auto-promote. Likely involves dependency analysis on the qcla_tree's output dataflow. Worklog: worklog/068_2026-05-15_h0ai_auto_self_reversing.md

title: Native Float32 soft-float arithmetic (24-bit mantissa)
description:
Follow-up to Bennett-3rph / U137 (closed 2026-05-01 by documenting the double-rounding deviation). The actual implementation work — native soft_f32_fadd / soft_fsub / soft_fmul / soft_fdiv operating directly on the 23-bit fraction + 8-bit biased exponent of binary32 — remains open as a future workstream.

Scope:
- soft_f32_fadd, soft_f32_fsub, soft_f32_fmul, soft_f32_fdiv, soft_f32_fneg, soft_f32_fma, soft_f32_fsqrt
- soft_f32_fcmp_oeq/olt/ole/... (full predicate table mirroring f64)
- Bit-exact-vs-Julia-Float32 test sweep (~1.2M random pairs, all subnormal/NaN/Inf/zero corners) per CLAUDE.md §13
- Wire f32 arithmetic in src/lowering/arith.jl + src/extract/instructions.jl to dispatch to native f32 callees instead of fpext→f64→fptrunc
- Add Float32 to _SUPPORTED_SCALAR_ARGS in src/Bennett.jl + a Float32 reversible_compile entrypoint mirroring the existing Float64 dispatcher (or unify both via the SoftFloat-struct + parametric width approach)

Rationale: hardware f32 single-rounds; the fpext→f64→fptrunc lowering double-rounds. Workloads that round-trip Float32 weights (ML inference, signal processing) get incorrect 1-ulp outputs at sticky-bit-edge inputs. Native paths would close the gap and let Bennett.jl honor the bit-exact contract for f32 too.

title: U204: QCLA n_anc guard W >= 4 unexplained
description:
Unified catalogue: U204 — reviews/2026-04-21/UNIFIED_CATALOGUE.md (LOW/NIT section)

Source reports: #14 F4
Files: reviews/2026-04-21/14_knuth.md

Sites: src/qcla.jl:43

Claim: QCLA n_anc guard W >= 4 unexplained.

Fix: Add a comment documenting why the QCLA path requires W >= 4 (derivation or citation).
close_reason: Added inline comment in src/qcla.jl explaining the W >= 4 ancilla-count guard. The formula W - popcount(W) - log2(W) gives n_anc = 0 for W in {1,2,3}: the QCLA reduces to a pure ripple path with no Xflat ancillae at small widths. W=4 (popW=1, T=2) gives n_anc=1 — the first non-trivial carry-tree level. Cite Draper-Kutin-Rains-Svore 2004 §4.1.

value: Bennett.jl test suite cost shape (measured 2026-05-23, JULIA_NUM_THREADS=32, --check-bounds=yes, commit 7bc1a3b): 688191 tests in 27m59s. 65% of wall-time = 12 test_*_llvm_*_dispatch.jl files (47-126s each, ~1100s total): eq9p/sfx9/s1zl/g82n/m2bv/3mo/emv/ky5n/bybh/0ulc/582/o7cy — all transcendental LLVM-intrinsic dispatch tests, all COMPILE-bound (reversible_compile builds 2.4M+-gate circuits, single-threaded; LLVM codegen uses a few threads internally, observed user/wall ≈ 2.7). Threading simulate sweeps is a MARGINAL win (single-digit seconds across the suite); softfloat random sweeps (test_softf*.jl) are 0.2-1.3s each, NOT the bottleneck despite their large iteration counts (95k+ in test_softfma). The 'thread the inner sweep' instinct is wrong for this codebase — gains require circuit caching across testsets (Bennett-hybr P2) or compile parallelism. simulate() itself is pure and trivially threadable when it IS the bottleneck (test_division 24.6s→22.4s).

Bennett-h0ai auto self_reversing has THREE layers to consider when triaging follow-ups: (Layer 1) producer-tag firing in arith.jl/tabulate.jl/etc, (Layer 2) _infer_self_reversing structural conditions in bennett_transform.jl:176 — including condition 4 that the boilerplate allowlist is only __pred_* / __ret_* / __branch_*, NOT IRCast groups even though casts are structurally self-reversing, (Layer 3) _validate_self_reversing! runtime probe. Triagers who stop at Layer 1 ship dead-effort code: e.g. firing the producer-tag on zext-fed widening mul has zero end-to-end effect because the surrounding zext cast groups disqualify the LR at Layer 2. The cast-group-as-boilerplate extension is a CORE 3+1 change to the h0ai contract. See worklog/069 jpa5 triage.

Long-term vision: Bennett.jl for all LLVM languages (C, C++, Rust, etc.) via raw bitcode input. Tracked as Bennett-xkv (P3, OPEN). User stated 2026-04-15: 'must not happen today.' — vision informs design decisions but is explicitly deferred. Practical implication for intrinsic coverage: raw LLVM inputs (non-Julia) won't have our SoftFloat wrapper, so sqrt/log/exp etc. will need DIRECT @llvm.sqrt.f64 → IRCall(soft_fsqrt) handlers in ir_extract.jl — not just SoftFloat dispatch. Current SoftFloat-routed path works for Julia-only inputs.

Triagers who stop at Layer 1 ship dead-effort code: e.g. firing the producer-tag on zext-fed widening mul has zero end-to-end effect because the surrounding zext cast groups disqualify the LR at Layer 2. The cast-group-as-boilerplate extension is a CORE 3+1 change to the h0ai contract. See worklog/069 jpa5 triage.

Advanced-arithmetic workstream (Sun-Borissov 2026 mul + Draper QCLA). Enzyme's coverage story (from Moses & Churavy NeurIPS 2020 + Enzyme.jl/Enzyme-MPI/Enzyme-GPU followups): covers all arithmetic, control flow, memory (load/store/alloca/GEP via shadow memory + TBAA), vectors, most intrinsics (sqrt/exp/log/sin/cos/pow/fma/etc.), function calls to LLVM-emitted code (recursive inline+differentiate). Hard stops: (1) inline assembly — callbr/asm! — no general semantic model; (2) external functions without source or @enzyme_custom_rule; (3) non-differentiable intrinsics like llvm.readcyclecounter / llvm.thread.pointer / llvm.returnaddress; (4) complex C++ EH (catchswitch/catchpad); (5) opaque FFI types; (6) coroutines / llvm.coro.*. Practical coverage ≈99% of pure numerical code; escape hatch is @enzyme_custom_rule / augmented_primal API. Bennett.jl's frontier is IDENTICAL: same hard stops (inline asm, external calls without source/rules), and register_callee! IS our @enzyme_custom_rule. Additional Bennett-specific concerns: concurrent atomic semantics (we'd collapse to single-thread), runtime exception paths (we'd need flag-wire model). North star: 'every LLVM opcode in pure numerical code from supported frontends, plus anything you write a rule for.'
